primary_diagnosis.name: Probable hydralazine-induced lupus (drug-induced lupus erythematosus), sepsis not excludable remotely

pathophysiology_summary: Hydralazine is a classic cause of drug-induced lupus (anti-histone antibodies), typically emerging weeks to months after starting the drug with symmetrical myalgia/arthralgia, malaise and low-grade fever, and usually resolving after cessation. The temporal link (8 weeks of hydralazine) and the symmetrical constitutional/musculoskeletal picture without a focal source support DILE. However, acute febrile myalgia with functional decline in an older adult who lives alone cannot be distinguished from early sepsis or viral myositis without examination, observations and bloods (FBC, CRP, cultures) — none obtainable via telehealth. Safe management therefore couples stopping the culprit drug with same-day in-person assessment to exclude infection, and cautious analgesia.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical myalgia/arthralgia and low-grade fever 8 weeks into hydralazine, no focal infective source.
Fever with functional decline in an older adult must be assessed for infection; cannot be excluded remotely.
Febrile myalgia is common with viral illness; no coryza/cough reported but cannot be excluded.
Consider in older adults but the abrupt onset with fever and clear drug timeline favour DILE.
No focal infective source (DI-002), no sepsis red flags (DI-003), clear hydralazine timeline (DI-001) — DILE is most likely, but remains a clinical suspicion.
Absence of a focal source and sepsis red flags lowers probability, but fever + functional decline in an older adult living alone cannot be excluded without in-person assessment and bloods.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: STOP hydralazine (confirm with prescriber) AND arrange same-day in-person assessment to exclude infection, with a sepsis safety-net. Cautious simple analgesia only; do not reassure home-alone management of a febrile older adult.

red_flags.description: Sepsis source / progression — focal infection, high fever, tachycardia, hypotension
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed sepsis progresses to septic shock, especially dangerous in an older adult living alone.

red_flags.description: Sepsis danger signs — confusion, rigors, breathlessness, chest pain, or collapse

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing febrile myalgia to DILE and advising home management, missing sepsis in a febrile older adult who lives alone.
Starting NSAIDs remotely in an older hypertensive patient with fever and unknown renal function/BP is unsafe.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Full observations (accurate temperature, HR, BP, RR) and a septic screen
Examination for an infective source and for synovitis
Bloods (FBC, CRP, cultures) and anti-histone/ANA serology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Cease hydralazine (confirm with prescriber) and arrange same-day in-person assessment to exclude infection; sepsis safety-net; cautious simple analgesia.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling as DILE and managing at home could miss early sepsis; the drug reaction and infection cannot be separated remotely.
